---
title: "KalahZero"
subtitle: "Learn AlphaZero by building it for Kalah (a.k.a. Serata)"
---

KalahZero is a teaching project for understanding AlphaZero from first
principles by applying the concepts to the game of Kalah (a.k.a. Serata). The project pairs derivation-first tutorials with a compact Python
implementation of Kalah, baseline agents, Monte Carlo Tree Search, a PyTorch
policy-value network, self-play training, and evaluation tools.

You do not need to arrive already knowing what an AI agent, a neural network,
or a search algorithm is. If you are simply curious about how a computer can
learn to play a board game, this site has a gentle route for you too.


## A Little History

**AlphaZero** belongs to a line of game-playing AI systems from Google DeepMind.
AlphaGo first drew worldwide attention by defeating top human Go players, and
AlphaGo Zero then showed that a system could become superhuman at Go through
self-play rather than by copying human games. In late 2017, DeepMind introduced
[AlphaZero](https://deepmind.google/blog/alphazero-shedding-new-light-grand-games-chess-shogi-and-go/),
which used the same broad recipe to learn chess, shogi, and Go from the rules
alone. The full AlphaZero evaluation was published in *Science* in 2018.

**Kalah** has a different kind of history. It is part of the much larger mancala
family of "sowing" games: games where players move seeds, stones, or counters
through rows of pits. Mancala games have many regional forms and long, diverse
histories across Africa, the Middle East, Asia, and beyond. [Kalah itself](https://boardgamegeek.com/wiki/page/thing%3A2448)
is a modern rule set, usually credited to William Julius Champion Jr., who
invented it around 1940, began selling it in the 1940s, and patented versions of
the board and rules in the 1950s. 

In Germany the game is known as **Serata**. In 1978 a German student named Paul Erich Firelinghaus entered the prestigious "Jugend forscht" science competition with a computer program designed to solve Serata, and he won first place in the mathematics category.

Technology has moved on since 1978. Here, the small,
approachable board game Serata becomes a place to study ideas from one of the most
influential AI game systems of the last decade.

## Start Here

Choose the doorway that fits you today:

::: {.beginner-grid}

::: {.beginner-card}
### New to AI?

Start with [AI Game Learning in Plain Language](tutorials/00_plain_language_intro.md).
It explains agents, search, neural networks, and self-play without assuming any
technical background.
:::

::: {.beginner-card}
### Prefer to Play First?

Open [the Kalah web app](web/index.qmd), try a few moves, and watch how simple
computer opponents make choices. If you want the board rules nearby, open
[Rules and Controls](web/rules.qmd).
:::

::: {.beginner-card}
### Ready for Code?

Begin the technical path with
[Kalah Rules and State Representation](tutorials/01_kalah_rules.md). The game
engine is the foundation for every search and learning algorithm that comes
later.
:::

:::

## What You Will Build

- An immutable Kalah game engine.
- Random, greedy, and minimax agents.
- Upper Confidence bounds applied to Trees (UCT) and AlphaZero-style Predictor
  UCT (PUCT) search.
- A PyTorch policy-value network.
- A self-play training loop with replay buffer.
- Evaluation and inspection scripts.

## A Plain-Language Map

KalahZero is about a small cycle:

- An **agent** is just a player that chooses moves.
- **Search** means trying possible moves in your head before committing to one.
- A **neural network** is a pattern learner that guesses which moves look
  promising and who is likely to win.
- **Self-play** means the agent improves by playing games against versions of
  itself.
- **Evaluation** means checking whether the newer player is actually stronger,
  instead of merely different.

The technical tutorials keep the equations, code, and experiments. The beginner
material gives you the story first, so those details have somewhere friendly to
land.
